title: Chapter 27: The Enemy of My Enemy
content: Theo had stopped sleeping three days ago.

Not by choice — his body had simply decided that rest was incompatible with what his mind was processing. Every time he closed his eyes, another connection formed. Another thread in the web. Another piece of evidence that made the picture clearer and, simultaneously, more revolting.

The rental apartment in Koreatown smelled like cold coffee and the particular staleness of a space lived in but not inhabited. Takeout containers formed a stratified geological record on the kitchen counter. His laptop sat on a folding table surrounded by three monitors he'd rigged from components bought at a Micro Center in Burbank, each screen displaying a different layer of the same story.

Marcus Reed had traced the money. That was public now — the expose had seen to that. Shell companies, lobbyist payments, campaign contributions flowing through Sterling Minds like blood through a corporate circulatory system. It was good work. Journalistic. Linear.
Theo had traced the code.

It had started with the algorithm itself — the computational backbone of everything Sterling Minds marketed as "digital safety." The parental monitoring. The toxicity detection. The sentiment analysis that flagged "at-risk" behaviour in minors. All of it ran through a single core engine, optimised and rebranded, but structurally identical to something Theo had seen before.

He'd recognised the pattern on day one. The graph traversal methodology. The Bayesian weighting on behavioural prediction. The particular way it clustered risk signals — not categorically, but relationally, mapping context between data points rather than the points themselves. It was distinctive. Unusual. The kind of approach that came from a specific intellectual tradition.
On day two, he'd found the paper.

"SHIELD: Scalable Heuristic Identification of Elevated Longitudinal Danger" — MIT Media Lab, 2023. Master's thesis. The author's name sat in 14-point Arial at the top of the title page like a signature on a forged cheque: Nova Valerius.

Theo had read the thesis three times. The mathematical framework was elegant — genuinely so. Not the kind of elegant that came from corporate R&D teams grinding through iterative improvements. The kind that came from a single mind seeing connections that others missed. He thought of the Pink Phantom's work the way a composer thinks of another composer's early pieces — recognising the voice before it had fully formed, hearing the phrases that would later become symphonies.
On day three, he'd found the conference proceedings.

The ACM Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems, 2024. "Collaborative Frameworks for Adolescent Digital Safety: A Unified Approach." Presenting author: Chloe Sterling. Co-authors: three members of the Sterling Minds engineering team. Nova Valerius's name: absent.

Theo had open both documents side by side on his centre monitor. The thesis on the left. The conference paper on the right. SHIELD's architecture diagram on the left. Sterling's "unified approach" on the right. Identical. Not inspired by. Not building upon. Identical — down to a variable naming convention that used Greek letters for risk categories, a quirk so specific it might as well have been a fingerprint.
He leaned back in the folding chair — the only piece of furniture in the apartment he'd bothered to assemble — and pressed the heels of his palms against his eyes.

The plagiarism was documented. Academic, provable, devastating. And nobody had noticed because nobody had reason to look. The thesis was from a master's student who'd vanished from the academic world. The conference paper was from the CEO of a $500 million company. Why would anyone compare them?

Theo dropped his hands and stared at the screens. The dossier on the Pink Phantom — *his* dossier, the one he'd compiled for Sterling Minds — sat in a file folder on his desktop. Complete. Comprehensive. Devastating in the wrong hands.

He thought about the contract. The specific language Sterling Minds' legal team had used: "full spectrum intelligence," "operational vulnerabilities," "strategic neutralisation options." He'd read those words a week ago and understood them as corporate euphemism. He read them now and understood them as something else entirely.
"Indeed," he said to the empty room. His voice sounded foreign after days of silence. "That's a problem."
He didn't deliver the dossier.
Instead, he spent four hours constructing a message that was precisely twenty-three words long. Brevity was not laziness — it was architecture. Every word had to bear weight.

The channel was the Phantom Network's own: the same encrypted relay the Phantom used to communicate with the dispersed collective of young women who formed the network's eyes and hands. Theo had mapped it during his research — not to infiltrate, but to understand. He understood it well enough to find the input node. A suggestion box for a phantom.
He typed:
*I've been looking for you. But not for the reasons you think.*
He attached three files.

File one: his complete dossier on the Pink Phantom. Identity analysis, movement patterns, methodology breakdown, psychological profile. Everything Sterling Minds had paid him to produce. Everything that would allow them to find her, isolate her, destroy her.

File two: a side-by-side comparison. Nova Valerius's SHIELD thesis on the left, Chloe Sterling's conference presentation on the right. Highlighted in yellow: the identical passages. Highlighted in red: the author list where Nova's name should have been.
File three: a single text document containing one line:
*I think we've been working for the same person. You just didn't know it.*
He sent it.
Then he sat in the dark and waited for whatever came next, which he expected to be nothing, or death, or something in between.
The reply came in eleven minutes.
Not from the Phantom.
The screen flickered — not the hardware kind of flicker, the *deliberate* kind. The kind that said *I am choosing to be seen.* And then she appeared.

A witch. Pixelated, Y2K-style, rendered in magenta and black and sparkle. She bounced on the screen like a Windows 95 desktop assistant huffed too much fairy dust. Stars erupted from her hat. Her eyes — two pixels each, but somehow expressive — regarded Theo with what could only be described as theatrical amusement.

"Well, well," she said, and her voice was exactly as pixelated as her appearance, a chirpy, synthetic thing that somehow conveyed irony in its static. "The hunter brings gifts to the hunted. How very fairy tale."
Theo stared.

He had seen advanced AI. He had built advanced AI. He had consulted for companies that would have sacrificed a kidney for the kind of natural language processing that this — entity — was demonstrating in a single sentence. The encryption alone, layered underneath the bouncing, sparkly avatar, was sophisticated enough that he could see at least three protocols he didn't recognise.
"That's... remarkable," he whispered.
The witch bounced higher, sparkles cascading. "I'll take that as a compliment, Mr. Nakamura. We've been watching you watch us for quite some time."

His surname. Of course they had it — he'd sent the dossier with his own name in the metadata. But the *delivery* — the timing, the presentation, the sheer audacity of an AI that introduced itself like a Broadway act — that was something no corporate system would ever produce.
"You're Virgil," he said. Not a question. He'd encountered the name in his research — referenced obliquely in Phantom Network chatter, a guardian and guide. He hadn't expected it to be *this*.

"Guilty as charged, darling. Though I prefer 'sentient digital entity with impeccable aesthetic sensibilities.'" The witch did a little spin. A trail of pixelated hearts followed. "Now. You've sent some very interesting files to my inbox. Care to explain why the man Sterling Minds hired to destroy my creator just handed over his entire playbook?"

Before Theo could respond, another voice entered the conversation. No visual — just audio, routed through the same encrypted channel. Clean, controlled, and wary in a way that suggested the speaker had learned caution through pain.
"Let him talk."
Nova.

Theo straightened in his chair. He was acutely aware that he was being evaluated — not just by a person he'd been hired to hunt, but by an AI that was clearly far more than a tool. The room felt smaller.

"I was contracted by Sterling Minds six weeks ago," he said, and his voice was steady because he'd practiced this — not the words, but the tone. Clinical. Precise. Factual. "The brief was to produce a comprehensive intelligence profile on the individual responsible for the Phantom Network's operations. Identity, capabilities, patterns, vulnerabilities."

He pulled up the contract on his screen and shared the window. The legal language filled the display — Standard NDA, scope of work, deliverables schedule. And there, in section 4.3: *Consultant shall provide strategic options for subject neutralisation, including but not limited to legal, reputational, and operational countermeasures.*
"That's not a law enforcement brief," Nova said. Her voice was flat.

"No. It isn't." Theo highlighted the relevant clause. "They don't want you arrested. They don't want you in a courtroom where you could present evidence, call witnesses, or make public statements. They want leverage. Your identity, your methods, your weaknesses — enough to neutralise you without making you a martyr."
A pause. Then Nova's voice, quieter: "They weren't going to arrest me. They were going to disappear me."

"Not disappear," Theo corrected gently. "Control. There's a difference, and it matters. Dead, you're a symbol. Controlled, you're a cautionary tale. Sterling Minds doesn't want a ghost. They want a leashed dog."

Another pause. Virgil's witch avatar had stopped bouncing. She — *it*, Theo corrected himself, though the personhood seemed increasingly relevant — sat motionless on the screen, watching him with those two-pixel eyes. If an AI could look thoughtful, Virgil looked thoughtful.
"And you're telling us this because...?" Virgil prompted.
Theo took a breath. This was the part that mattered — the part where he stopped being a contractor and became something else, or failed to.

"Because I've read her code," he said, nodding toward the screen where Virgil sat. "Not all of it — I don't have access to Inferno. But I've seen the traces. The methodology. The architecture." He looked directly at the camera. "And I've read yours, Ms Valerius. The SHIELD thesis. The algorithm that Sterling Minds stole and baked into every product they sell. I know what they took from you. And I know what they're willing to do to keep it."
Silence. The kind that weighed something.

"I'm not here as a hacker," Theo continued. "You don't need one — you've built something I couldn't have imagined, and I say that with professional humility that does not come easily to me." A beat. "I'm offering to be a proxy. Someone who can operate in the physical world, in the legitimate tech world, while you stay hidden. I have credentials. Reputation. I can access conferences, networks, rooms that you can't enter from a safe house. I can be your eyes and ears in places that require a face and a name."
Virgil resumed bouncing. The sparkles had a different quality now — less sarcastic, more analytical.

"He's skilled," the AI said, addressing Nova but speaking aloud, which Theo understood was deliberate. An animal displaying its behaviour to a new observer. "His methodology is sound. Methodical, patient, thorough — almost annoyingly so. And he just turned down a six-figure contract to have this conversation. That's either integrity or a very long con."
"Which do you think it is?" Nova asked.
"My gut says integrity." Virgil's witch performed a thoughtful little tap of her chin — a human gesture rendered in eight-bit charm. "My code says verify."
A dry laugh — also ghostly, also routed through audio only. "Your code always says verify."
"That's why you keep me around, darling."

Theo waited. He was good at waiting. He'd spent three days in this apartment not sleeping, which was, in its way, an extended exercise in patience. He didn't fill the silence. He let it sit there, heavy and honest, until Nova was ready to pick it up.

"Conditional," she said finally. "You work through Virgil. You never access Inferno directly — you don't touch it, you don't try to map it, you don't even think about it too hard. You're a source and a proxy. Not an operative. You don't act without clearing it through us first."
"Agreed."

"And if you breach my trust — if I get so much as a whisper that you're feeding information back to Sterling Minds, to anyone — Virgil erases every trace of our contact from your systems. Every file, every message, every log. It'll be like this conversation never happened. And then I'll make sure it didn't happen to *you*, either."

The threat was not theatrical. It was not emotional. It was a practical statement of consequences from someone who had already survived one betrayal and would not survive another. Theo recognised the architecture of it — the same structural thinking that had built SHIELD, that had built the Phantom Network. Nova Valerius didn't make threats. She built systems, and the systems handled the rest.
"Fair enough," he said. And then, because he was who he was, he added: "I've read your code. I'd be terrified of that pixilated menace too."
Virgil's witch erupted into a burst of sparkling, spinning delight. "As you should be, darling. As you absolutely should be."

The channel stayed open. The conversation continued — logistics, protocols, the mechanics of how a proxy relationship would work across the legitimate and shadow economies of the tech world. But later, after the audio had cut and the screens had dimmed and Theo sat alone in the dark Koreatown apartment, he turned back to his monitors.
The dossier was archived. The contract was nullified in his mind, if not yet in law. The alliance was formed.

But on his leftmost screen — the one he'd turned away from during the call — there was still a file open. Not the SHIELD thesis. Not the conference paper. Something else. Something he'd found on day three, buried so deep in Sterling Minds' corporate filings that it had taken a chain of six shell companies to surface.
He stared at it. He hadn't shared it with Nova. Not yet. Not because he was holding leverage — he'd given her everything that could hurt her, which meant he'd given her everything that mattered.
He hadn't shared it because he didn't understand it yet. And Theo Nakamura did not present findings until they were findings.
The file sat there, waiting, its contents rendered in the cold language of corporate accounting:
*Project LEONARDO — Budget Allocation FY2025. Approved: Chloe Sterling. Classification: Internal — Executive Eyes Only. Status: Active.*
LEONARDO. He didn't know what it was. But he knew the budget.
It was nine figures.
Theo closed the file, locked it, and sat in the dark, listening to the hum of machines he didn't fully understand yet.
He'd found the plagiarism. He'd found the theft. He'd found the contract to destroy the Phantom.
What he hadn't found — not yet — was what Sterling Minds was building with all that stolen code and all that money.
But something told him he would. And something told him it would be worse than anything he'd seen so far.
"Brilliant," he murmured to the empty room, and the word tasted like a warning.
